title: Remove duplicate rows — deduplicate
text: Removes duplicate rows data frame based specified ID column, retaining complete row (fewest NA values) per ID group. record duplicate groups attached result attribute.

title: Remove duplicate rows — deduplicate
code: deduplicate(data, id_col, drop_na = FALSE)

title: Remove duplicate rows — deduplicate
text: data data frame. id_col Column name ID column use duplicate detection, supplied either unquoted (id) quoted ("id"). drop_na Logical. TRUE, rows ID column NA dropped de-duplication. Default FALSE.

title: Remove duplicate rows — deduplicate
text: data frame one row retained per unique value id_col, chosen maximum row completeness (fewest NAs across columns). original duplicate groups accessible via attr(result, "duplicates"), data frame containing rows part duplicate group, additional logical column .kept_row indicating row retained.

title: Remove duplicate rows — deduplicate
text: Row completeness computed count non-NA values across columns using rowSums(!.na(data)). multiple rows tie completeness, .max() retains first occurrence. Progress messages printed console reporting number NA ID rows removed (drop_na = TRUE) total number duplicate rows removed.

title: Remove duplicate rows — deduplicate
code: df <- data.frame(   id    = c(1, 2, 2, 3, 3),   name  = c("A", "B", NA, "C", "C"),   score = c(90, 85, 85, 78, 78) )  # Retain the most complete row per ID deduplicate(df, id_col = id) #> [deduplicate] 2 duplicate row(s) removed #>   id name score #> 1  1    A    90 #> 2  2    B    85 #> 4  3    C    78  # Inspect which rows were flagged as duplicates result <- deduplicate(df, id_col = id) #> [deduplicate] 2 duplicate row(s) removed attr(result, "duplicates") #>   id name score .kept_row #> 2  2    B    85      TRUE #> 3  2 <NA>    85     FALSE #> 4  3    C    78      TRUE #> 5  3    C    78     FALSE  # Drop rows where the ID itself is NA before deduplication df_na <- data.frame(   id    = c(1, NA, 2, 2),   value = c("a", "b", "c", "d") ) deduplicate(df_na, id_col = id, drop_na = TRUE) #> [deduplicate] 1 NA row(s) removed from ID column #> [deduplicate] 1 duplicate row(s) removed #>   id value #> 1  1     a #> 3  2     c

what: Duplicate rows by a count column — duplicate
title: Duplicate rows by a count column — duplicate
text: Expands data frame repeating row X number times, specified count column. Useful reconstructing individual-level data aggregated frequency-weighted data frames.

title: Duplicate rows by a count column — duplicate
code: duplicate(data, n_col, drop_na = FALSE)

title: Duplicate rows by a count column — duplicate
text: data data frame. n_col Column name column containing duplication counts, supplied either unquoted (n) quoted ("n"). Rows NA counts counted unless drop_na = TRUE. drop_na Logical. TRUE, rows n_col NA dropped expansion. Default FALSE.

title: Duplicate rows by a count column — duplicate
text: data frame rows data, row appears n_col[] times (n_col[] NA drop_na = FALSE). Row names reset. n_col column retained output.

title: Duplicate rows by a count column — duplicate
text: Expansion performed via rep(seq_len(nrow(data)), times = n_col), original row order preserved within group duplicates. NA counts replaced 1 prior expansion drop_na = FALSE. console message reports final row count expanded data frame.

title: Duplicate rows by a count column — duplicate
code: df <- data.frame(   group = c("A", "B", "C"),   value = c(10, 20, 30),   n     = c(3, 1, 2) )  # Expand so each row repeats n times duplicate(df, n_col = n) #> [duplicate] dataset expanded to 6 rows based on 'n' #>     group value n #> 1       A    10 3 #> 1.1     A    10 3 #> 1.2     A    10 3 #> 2       B    20 1 #> 3       C    30 2 #> 3.1     C    30 2  # NA counts default to 1 repetition df_na <- data.frame(   group = c("A", "B", "C"),   n     = c(2, NA, 3) ) duplicate(df_na, n_col = n) #> [duplicate] dataset expanded to 6 rows based on 'n' #>     group n #> 1       A 2 #> 1.1     A 2 #> 2       B 1 #> 3       C 3 #> 3.1     C 3 #> 3.2     C 3  # Drop rows with NA counts instead duplicate(df_na, n_col = n, drop_na = TRUE) #> [duplicate] 1 NA row(s) removed from numbering column #> [duplicate] dataset expanded to 5 rows based on 'n' #>     group n #> 1       A 2 #> 1.1     A 2 #> 3       C 3 #> 3.1     C 3 #> 3.2     C 3

what: Format taxonomic names for italic display in ggplot2 — italicize
title: Format taxonomic names for italic display in ggplot2 — italicize
text: Converts taxonomic names one columns plotmath italic expressions suitable use ggplot2 axis labels legends via ggplot2::label_parsed. new column named <column>_italic appended data frame input column.

title: Format taxonomic names for italic display in ggplot2 — italicize
code: italicize(data, columns, drop_na = FALSE)

title: Format taxonomic names for italic display in ggplot2 — italicize
text: data data frame. columns Column name c() column names italicize, supplied either unquoted (SciName) quoted ("SciName"). named column must contain character strings (e.g. "Homo sapiens"). drop_na Logical. TRUE, rows NA specified columns dropped conversion. Default FALSE.

title: Format taxonomic names for italic display in ggplot2 — italicize
text: input data frame one additional character column appended per entry columns, named <column>_italic. new column contains plotmath expression form "italic(Genus~species)", spaces replaced ~ preserve word spacing rendered. NA values source column remain NA output column drop_na = FALSE.

title: Format taxonomic names for italic display in ggplot2 — italicize
text: _italic columns intended mapped ggplot2 aesthetic (e.g. aes(x = Species_italic)) rendered parsed expressions passing label_parsed labels argument corresponding scale. keeps names plain character data plot rendered, avoiding manual expression() calls. Authorship strings appended taxon_cite format "Genus species (Author, Year)" automatically detected rendered roman type alongside italic canonical name, producing expressions form italic("Genus species")~"(Author, Year)". Spaces names replaced ~ prior wrapping italic(), required plotmath render multi-word names (e.g. genus + species) correctly.

title: Format taxonomic names for italic display in ggplot2 — italicize
code: df <- data.frame(   SciName = c("Homo sapiens", "Panthera leo", "Canis lupus"),   count   = c(120, 45, 78) )  # Italicize a single column df <- italicize(df, SciName) df$SciName_italic #> [1] "italic(\"Homo sapiens\")" "italic(\"Panthera leo\")" #> [3] "italic(\"Canis lupus\")"   # Use in a ggplot2 axis with parsed labels if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ggplot(df, aes(x = SciName_italic, y = count)) +   geom_col() +   scale_x_discrete(labels = ggplot2::label_parsed) } # }  # Italicize multiple columns at once df2 <- data.frame(   genus   = c("Homo", "Panthera"),   species = c("sapiens", "leo") ) italicize(df2, c(genus, species)) #>      genus species       genus_italic    species_italic #> 1     Homo sapiens     italic("Homo") italic("sapiens") #> 2 Panthera     leo italic("Panthera")     italic("leo")  # Drop rows where the name column is NA df_na <- data.frame(   SciName = c("Homo sapiens", NA, "Canis lupus"),   count   = c(10, 5, 8) ) italicize(df_na, SciName, drop_na = TRUE) #>        SciName count         SciName_italic #> 1 Homo sapiens    10 italic("Homo sapiens") #> 3  Canis lupus     8  italic("Canis lupus")

what: Identify coordinate columns in a data frame — latlong_column
title: Identify coordinate columns in a data frame — latlong_column
text: Detects latitude, longitude, combined coordinate columns based value ranges format matching. Supports decimal degree, DMS (DD°MM′SS″), base-60 (DD°MM′) coordinate formats. Returns named list elements combined, latitude, longitude, containing names indices detected columns.

title: Identify coordinate columns in a data frame — latlong_column
code: latlong_column(data, sep = ",")

title: Identify coordinate columns in a data frame — latlong_column
text: data data frame search coordinate columns. sep Character. Separator used split combined coordinate columns (.e. columns containing latitude longitude single string). Default ",".

title: Identify coordinate columns in a data frame — latlong_column
text: named list three elements: combined Columns containing latitude longitude     single delimited string (e.g. "51.5,-0.1"). latitude Columns containing latitude values . Numeric     range constrained [-90, 90]. longitude Columns containing longitude values . Numeric     range constrained [-180, 180]. Columns already assigned     combined latitude excluded. element named list mapping column name column index data. empty named list (list()) returned coordinate type detected.

title: Identify coordinate columns in a data frame — latlong_column
text: Detection follows priority order: combined columns identified first, latitude, longitude. column can appear one category — latitude candidates excluded longitude, combined candidates excluded . three recognised coordinate formats : Decimal degrees: "-12.345" "51.5" DMS: "12°34′56″N" Base-60: "12°34′N" Detection based value content, column names, columns non-standard names (e.g. "point_y") still detected provided values match recognized coordinate format pass range check. least one valid coordinate value required column detected.

title: Identify coordinate columns in a data frame — latlong_column
code: df <- data.frame(   id  = 1:6,   lat = c(51.5, 48.8, 40.7, 35.6, -33.9, 55.8),   lon = c(-0.1, 2.3, -74.0, 139.7, 151.2, 37.6) )  # Detect separate latitude and longitude columns latlong_column(df) #> $combined #> named list() #>  #> $latitude #> $latitude$id #> [1] 1 #>  #> $latitude$lat #> [1] 2 #>  #>  #> $longitude #> $longitude$lon #> [1] 3 #>  #>   # Access detected column names and indices result <- latlong_column(df) result$latitude #> $id #> [1] 1 #>  #> $lat #> [1] 2 #>  result$longitude #> $lon #> [1] 3 #>   # Detect a combined coordinate column df2 <- data.frame(   id     = 1:6,   coords = c("51.5,-0.1", "48.8,2.3", "40.7,-74.0",              "35.6,139.7", "-33.9,151.2", "55.8,37.6") ) latlong_column(df2) #> $combined #> $combined$coords #> [1] 2 #>  #>  #> $latitude #> $latitude$id #> [1] 1 #>  #>  #> $longitude #> named list() #>   # Use a custom separator for combined columns df3 <- data.frame(   coords = c("51.5;-0.1", "48.8;2.3", "40.7;-74.0",              "35.6;139.7", "-33.9;151.2", "55.8;37.6") ) latlong_column(df3, sep = ";") #> $combined #> $combined$coords #> [1] 1 #>  #>  #> $latitude #> named list() #>  #> $longitude #> named list() #>

what: Combine separate coordinate columns into one — latlong_combine
title: Combine separate coordinate columns into one — latlong_combine
text: Merges separate latitude longitude columns single combined coordinate column, appended data frame. inverse operation latlong_split. Note combined columns accepted functions latlong_range latlong_region; use latlong_split separate columns filtering.

title: Combine separate coordinate columns into one — latlong_combine
code: latlong_combine(   data,   latitude,   longitude,   new_column = "latlong",   sep = ", ",   drop_na = FALSE )

title: Combine separate coordinate columns into one — latlong_combine
text: data data frame containing coordinate columns. latitude Column name latitude column, supplied either unquoted (lat) quoted ("lat"). Required combined_col provided. longitude Column name longitude column, supplied either unquoted (lon) quoted ("lon"). Required combined_col provided. new_column Column name new combined column, supplied either unquoted (latlong) quoted ("latlong"). Default latlong. sep Character. Separator inserted latitude longitude values combined column. Default ", ". Must match sep argument latlong_split combined column split later. drop_na Logical. TRUE, rows either latitude longitude column NA dropped combining. Default FALSE.

title: Combine separate coordinate columns into one — latlong_combine
text: input data frame one additional character column appended, named according new_column, containing values form "<latitude><sep><longitude>". original latitude longitude columns retained. drop_na = FALSE, rows NA either coordinate column produce "NA<sep>NA" "<value><sep>NA" combined column.

title: Combine separate coordinate columns into one — latlong_combine
text: coordinate columns coerced character concatenation via paste0(), numeric, integer, character coordinate columns accepted. validation coordinate ranges formats performed; use latlong_format check column formats combining. console message reports number rows removed drop_na = TRUE.

title: Combine separate coordinate columns into one — latlong_combine
code: df <- data.frame(   id  = 1:4,   lat = c(51.5, 48.8, 40.7, 35.6),   lon = c(-0.1, 2.3, -74.0, 139.7) )  # Combine with default separator and column name latlong_combine(df, latitude = lat, longitude = lon) #>   id  lat   lon     latlong #> 1  1 51.5  -0.1  51.5, -0.1 #> 2  2 48.8   2.3   48.8, 2.3 #> 3  3 40.7 -74.0   40.7, -74 #> 4  4 35.6 139.7 35.6, 139.7  # Use a custom separator and column name latlong_combine(df, latitude = lat, longitude = lon,                 new_column = coords, sep = ";") #>   id  lat   lon     coords #> 1  1 51.5  -0.1  51.5;-0.1 #> 2  2 48.8   2.3   48.8;2.3 #> 3  3 40.7 -74.0   40.7;-74 #> 4  4 35.6 139.7 35.6;139.7  # Drop rows where either coordinate is NA df_na <- data.frame(   lat = c(51.5, NA, 40.7),   lon = c(-0.1, 2.3, NA) ) latlong_combine(df_na, latitude = lat, longitude = lon, drop_na = TRUE) #> [latlong_combine] 2 NA row(s) removed #>    lat  lon    latlong #> 1 51.5 -0.1 51.5, -0.1

title: Convert coordinate formats — latlong_convert
text: Converts coordinate columns decimal degrees, DMS (DD°MM′SS″), base-60 (DD°MM′) formats. Handles separate latitude longitude columns well combined coordinate columns. Columns converted place; combined columns may optionally split separate latitude longitude columns.

title: Convert coordinate formats — latlong_convert
code: latlong_convert(   data,   column,   convert = "decimal",   type = "auto",   drop_na = FALSE,   split_combined = FALSE )

title: Convert coordinate formats — latlong_convert
text: data data frame containing coordinate columns. column Column name c() column names convert, supplied either unquoted (lat) quoted ("lat"). convert Character. Target coordinate format. One "decimal" (default), "dms", "base60". type Character. Coordinate type used direction suffix assignment DMS base-60 output. One "auto" (default), "lat", "lon". "auto", type inferred column name (matching "lat" "lon"/"lng"/"long") value range name uninformative. drop_na Logical. TRUE, rows converted column contains NA dropped conversion. Default FALSE. split_combined Logical. TRUE, combined coordinate columns split two new columns named <column>_lat <column>_lon rather kept single combined column. Default FALSE.

title: Convert coordinate formats — latlong_convert
text: input data frame converted coordinate columns. non-combined columns, original column overwritten converted values. combined columns, behavior depends split_combined: FALSE, combined column overwritten converted     values delimited format. TRUE, two new columns appended — <column>_lat     <column>_lon — original column retained. convert = "decimal", output columns numeric. "dms" "base60", output columns character.

title: Convert coordinate formats — latlong_convert
text: input formats first parsed decimal degrees internally conversion. parser handles decimal, DMS, base-60 formats, inferring sign cardinal direction suffixes (S, W) sign degree value. Zero-width BOM characters stripped parsing. Combined columns detected automatically presence two numeric components single value. separator used split combined columns assumed ","; use latlong_split custom separator needed converting. Direction suffixes (N, S, E, W) appended DMS base-60 output type can determined. type = "auto" type inferred column name value range, suffix added. Use latlong_format check input formats conversion, latlong_filter remove --range values beforehand.

title: Convert coordinate formats — latlong_convert
code: df <- data.frame(   id  = 1:4,   lat = c(51.5, 48.8, -33.9, 40.7),   lon = c(-0.1, 2.3, 151.2, -74.0) )  # Convert decimal columns to DMS latlong_convert(df, c(lat, lon), convert = "dms") #> [latlong_convert] NA rows removed: 0 #>   id        lat         lon #> 1  1  51°30′0″N     0°6′0″W #> 2  2 48°47′60″N   2°17′60″E #> 3  3 33°53′60″S 151°11′60″E #> 4  4  40°42′0″N    74°0′0″W  # Convert to base-60 latlong_convert(df, c(lat, lon), convert = "base60") #> [latlong_convert] NA rows removed: 0 #>   id     lat      lon #> 1  1 51°30′N    0°6′W #> 2  2 48°48′N   2°18′E #> 3  3 33°54′S 151°12′E #> 4  4 40°42′N   74°0′W  # Convert a single column, specifying coordinate type explicitly latlong_convert(df, lat, convert = "dms", type = "lat") #> [latlong_convert] NA rows removed: 0 #>   id        lat   lon #> 1  1  51°30′0″N  -0.1 #> 2  2 48°47′60″N   2.3 #> 3  3 33°53′60″S 151.2 #> 4  4  40°42′0″N -74.0  # Convert a combined column and keep as single column df_combined <- data.frame(   coords = c("51.5,-0.1", "48.8,2.3", "-33.9,151.2") ) latlong_convert(df_combined, coords, convert = "dms") #> [latlong_convert] NA rows removed: 0 #>                    coords #> 1      51°30′0″N, 0°6′0″W #> 2   48°47′60″N, 2°17′60″E #> 3 33°53′60″S, 151°11′60″E  # Convert a combined column and split into separate lat/lon columns latlong_convert(df_combined, coords, convert = "decimal",                 split_combined = TRUE) #> [latlong_convert] NA rows removed: 0 #>        coords coords_lat coords_lon #> 1   51.5,-0.1       51.5       -0.1 #> 2    48.8,2.3       48.8        2.3 #> 3 -33.9,151.2      -33.9      151.2  # Drop rows that produce NA after conversion df_na <- data.frame(   lat = c(51.5, NA, 40.7),   lon = c(-0.1, 2.3, -74.0) ) latlong_convert(df_na, c(lat, lon), convert = "dms", drop_na = TRUE) #> [latlong_convert] NA rows removed: 1 #>         lat      lon #> 1 51°30′0″N  0°6′0″W #> 3 40°42′0″N 74°0′0″W

what: Filter rows by real-world coordinate validity — latlong_filter
title: Filter rows by real-world coordinate validity — latlong_filter
text: Removes rows coordinates fall outside valid geographic ranges ([-90, 90] latitude, [-180, 180] longitude). Accepts either separate latitude longitude columns, single combined coordinate column. Supports decimal degree, DMS (DD°MM′SS″), base-60 (DD°MM′) coordinate formats.

title: Filter rows by real-world coordinate validity — latlong_filter
code: latlong_filter(   data,   latitude = NULL,   longitude = NULL,   combined_col = NULL,   sep = ",",   drop_na = FALSE )

title: Filter rows by real-world coordinate validity — latlong_filter
text: data data frame containing coordinate columns. latitude Optional. Column name latitude column, supplied either unquoted (lat) quoted ("lat"). Required combined_col provided. longitude Optional. Column name longitude column, supplied either unquoted (lon) quoted ("lon"). Required combined_col provided. combined_col Optional. Column name combined coordinate column containing latitude longitude single delimited string (e.g. "51.5,-0.1"), supplied either unquoted (coords) quoted ("coords"). Required latitude longitude provided. sep Character. Separator used split combined_col latitude longitude parts. Default ",". drop_na Logical. TRUE, rows NA either coordinate dropped addition --range rows. Default FALSE.

title: Filter rows by real-world coordinate validity — latlong_filter
text: data frame containing rows valid coordinates, columns data. Removed rows attached attr(result, "invalid") inspection. console message reports total number rows removed.

title: Filter rows by real-world coordinate validity — latlong_filter
text: coordinate formats parsed decimal degrees internally range validation. parser handles decimal, DMS, base-60 formats, inferring sign cardinal direction suffixes (S, W) sign degree value. Zero-width BOM characters stripped parsing. Either combined_col latitude longitude must provided; supplying neither raises error. drop_na = FALSE (default), rows NA coordinates still removed pass range validation, captured attr(result, "invalid"). Use latlong_format check coordinate formats filtering, latlong_column identify coordinate columns names known advance.

title: Filter rows by real-world coordinate validity — latlong_filter
code: df <- data.frame(   id  = 1:5,   lat = c(51.5, 48.8, 91.0, -33.9, NA),   lon = c(-0.1, 2.3, 139.7, 151.2, 37.6) )  # Filter using separate latitude and longitude columns latlong_filter(df, latitude = lat, longitude = lon) #> [latlong_filter] 2 row(s) removed with invalid or out-of-range coordinates #>   id   lat   lon #> 1  1  51.5  -0.1 #> 2  2  48.8   2.3 #> 4  4 -33.9 151.2  # Inspect rows that were removed result <- latlong_filter(df, latitude = lat, longitude = lon) #> [latlong_filter] 2 row(s) removed with invalid or out-of-range coordinates attr(result, "invalid") #>   id lat   lon #> 3  3  91 139.7 #> 5  5  NA  37.6  # Also drop rows where either coordinate is NA latlong_filter(df, latitude = lat, longitude = lon, drop_na = TRUE) #> [latlong_filter] 2 row(s) removed with invalid or out-of-range coordinates #>   id   lat   lon #> 1  1  51.5  -0.1 #> 2  2  48.8   2.3 #> 4  4 -33.9 151.2  # Filter using a combined coordinate column df_combined <- data.frame(   id     = 1:4,   coords = c("51.5,-0.1", "91.0,2.3", "-33.9,151.2", "48.8,181.0") ) latlong_filter(df_combined, combined_col = coords) #> [latlong_filter] 2 row(s) removed with invalid or out-of-range coordinates #>   id      coords #> 1  1   51.5,-0.1 #> 3  3 -33.9,151.2  # Combined column with a custom separator df_sep <- data.frame(   coords = c("51.5;-0.1", "91.0;2.3", "-33.9;151.2") ) latlong_filter(df_sep, combined_col = coords, sep = ";") #> [latlong_filter] 1 row(s) removed with invalid or out-of-range coordinates #>        coords #> 1   51.5;-0.1 #> 3 -33.9;151.2

what: Check the format of coordinate columns — latlong_format
title: Check the format of coordinate columns — latlong_format
text: Detects reports coordinate format – decimal degrees, DMS (DDdeg.MM'SS''}), base-60 (\code{DDdeg.MM') – values one columns. Combined columns (latitude longitude stored single delimited string) split format detection. Results returned named list, one element per column.

title: Check the format of coordinate columns — latlong_format
code: latlong_format(data, columns, sep = ",", drop_na = TRUE)

title: Check the format of coordinate columns — latlong_format
text: data data frame containing coordinate columns. columns Column name c() column names check, supplied either unquoted (lat) quoted ("lat"). sep Character. Separator used split combined coordinate columns format detection. Default ",". drop_na Logical. TRUE, values match recognized coordinate format excluded summarizing results. Default TRUE.

title: Check the format of coordinate columns — latlong_format
text: named list one element per column columns. element named list two components: format Character vector detected format names present     column. One "decimal", "dms",     "base60". Returns "unknown" values match     recognised format (values excluded drop_na). counts Integer vector length format,     giving number values matching detected format.

title: Check the format of coordinate columns — latlong_format
text: three recognised coordinate formats : Decimal degrees: "-12.345" "51.5" DMS: "12deg.34'56''N" Base-60: "12deg.34'N" column may return multiple formats values inconsistently formatted – example, mix decimal DMS entries. reported rather resolved, allowing user decide handle mixed formats passing columns latlong_combine latlong_split. Combined columns (containing sep) detected automatically split format checking, sep used latlong_combine latlong_split passed consistent results.

title: Check the format of coordinate columns — latlong_format
code: df <- data.frame(   id  = 1:4,   lat = c("51.5", "48.8", "40.7", "35.6"),   lon = c("-0.1", "2.3", "-74.0", "139.7") )  # Check format of a single column latlong_format(df, lat) #> $lat #> $lat$format #> [1] "decimal" #>  #> $lat$counts #> [1] 4 #>  #>   # Check multiple columns at once latlong_format(df, c(lat, lon)) #> $lat #> $lat$format #> [1] "decimal" #>  #> $lat$counts #> [1] 4 #>  #>  #> $lon #> $lon$format #> [1] "decimal" #>  #> $lon$counts #> [1] 4 #>  #>   # Mixed formats in one column df_mixed <- data.frame(   coords = c("51.5", "48deg.52'N", "40.7", "35deg.36'00''N") ) latlong_format(df_mixed, coords) #> $coords #> $coords$format #> [1] "decimal" "dms"     "base60"  #>  #> $coords$counts #> [1] 2 1 1 #>  #>   # Combined latitude-longitude column with custom separator df_combined <- data.frame(   latlon = c("51.5;-0.1", "48.8;2.3", "40.7;-74.0") ) latlong_format(df_combined, latlon, sep = ";") #> $latlon #> $latlon$format #> [1] "decimal" #>  #> $latlon$counts #> [1] 6 #>  #>   # Include unknown-format values in counts df_dirty <- data.frame(   lat = c("51.5", "not_a_coord", "40.7", NA) ) latlong_format(df_dirty, lat, drop_na = FALSE) #> $lat #> $lat$format #> [1] "decimal" #>  #> $lat$counts #> [1] 2 #>  #>

title: Assign hemispheres to coordinates — latlong_hemisphere
text: Appends NS_hemisphere EW_hemisphere columns data frame based sign coordinate values. Accepts either separate latitude longitude columns single combined coordinate column. Supports decimal degree, DMS (DD°MM′SS″), base-60 (DD°MM′) coordinate formats.

title: Assign hemispheres to coordinates — latlong_hemisphere
code: latlong_hemisphere(   data,   latitude = NULL,   longitude = NULL,   combined_col = NULL,   drop_na = FALSE )

title: Assign hemispheres to coordinates — latlong_hemisphere
text: data data frame containing coordinate columns. latitude Optional. Column name latitude column, supplied either unquoted (lat) quoted ("lat"). Required combined_col provided. longitude Optional. Column name longitude column, supplied either unquoted (lon) quoted ("lon"). Required combined_col provided. combined_col Optional. Column name combined coordinate column containing latitude longitude single comma-delimited string (e.g. "51.5,-0.1"), supplied either unquoted (coords) quoted ("coords").
